  • Learn More book
  • Quizzes info
  • Contact Us phone



Featured Flower: Protea

The Protea, often referred to as the "King of Flowers," is a symbol of change and hope.

Native to South Africa, it represents diversity and courage. These ancient flowers have existed for over 300 million years!

Featured Flower: Daisy

Daisies symbolize innocence and purity, bringing a touch of simplicity to any garden.

Originating from Europe and Africa, the name "Daisy" comes from "day's eye" because the flower head closes at night.

Featured Flower: Sunflower

Sunflowers are known for their "heliotropism," meaning they turn their faces to follow the sun.

Native to North America, a single sunflower can contain up to 2,000 seeds and is a vital source of energy.

Featured Flower: Rose

The Rose is the universal symbol of love and passion, adored for its fragrance and elegance.

While found globally, most species are native to Asia. Fossil evidence shows they have been around for 35 million years!

Featured Flower: Lavender

Known for its calming scent, Lavender is a staple in gardens meant for relaxation and peace.

Native to the Mediterranean coast, ancient Romans used it to scent their baths.

Featured Flower: Orchid

Orchids represent luxury and strength, boasting some of the most intricate designs in nature.

Found Worldwide (primarily Tropical regions), Orchids are one of the largest plant families in existence.

Featured Flower: Tulip

Tulips are the heralds of spring, symbolizing perfect love and elegance.

Originally native to Central Asia and Turkey, they were once more valuable than gold in the Netherlands.

Featured Flower: Lily

Lilies are majestic blooms often associated with rebirth and motherhood.

Native to temperate regions of the Northern Hemisphere, these flowers are beautiful but toxic to cats.

Featured Flower: Cherry Blossom

The Sakura represents the fleeting beauty of life and the arrival of spring.

Native to East Asia (Japan, China, Korea), the practice of "Hanami" involves picnicking under these trees.

Featured Flower: Peony

Known as the "King of Flowers" in China, Peonies symbolize bashfulness and prosperity.

Native to Asia, Europe, and Western North America, they can bloom for over 100 years.

Featured Flower: Marigold

Marigolds are vibrant, hardy flowers often associated with the warmth of the sun.

Native to Mexico and Central America, they are essential to "Dia de los Muertos" celebrations.

Featured Flower: Hydrangea

These lush, ball-shaped clusters represent heartfelt emotions and gratitude.

Native to Asia and the Americas, their color changes based on the pH of the soil.

Featured Flower: Lotus

The Lotus is a sacred symbol of purity and enlightenment, rising clean from muddy waters.

Native to Central and Southeast Asia, its seeds can remain viable for over a thousand years.

Featured Flower: Hibiscus

Tropical and striking, the Hibiscus is often associated with "delicate beauty."

Native to warm-temperate and tropical regions (primarily Asia), its flowers are often used to make tea.

Featured Flower: Daffodil

The bright yellow Narcissus represents new beginnings and unparalleled creativity.

Native to Europe and North Africa, tradition says a gift of daffodils ensures happiness.

Featured Flower: Dahlia

With stunning geometric petals, Dahlias signify inner strength and dignity.

Native to Mexico and Central America, they are the national flower of Mexico.

Featured Flower: Jasmine

Famous for its intoxicating fragrance, Jasmine represents love and sensuality.

Native to tropical and subtropical Eurasia and Australasia, it is nicknamed "Queen of the Night."

Featured Flower: Carnation

Carnations are versatile blooms that express fascination and divine love.

Native to the Mediterranean region, their scientific name translates to "Flower of the Gods."

Featured Flower: Bluebell

These woodland flowers are symbols of humility, constancy, and everlasting love.

Native to Atlantic regions of Western Europe, they are often linked to fairy folklore.

Featured Flower: Iris

Named after the Greek goddess of the rainbow, the Iris represents wisdom and hope.

Native to temperate regions of Europe and Asia, the petals represent faith and valor.

Featured Flower: Poppy

Poppies are deeply symbolic of remembrance and consolation.

Native to Europe and Asia, they are famous for blooming in disturbed battlefield soil.

Featured Flower: Chrysanthemum

These "Mums" symbolize longevity, joy, and optimism.

Native to East Asia (primarily China), they are the symbol of the Japanese Emperor.

Featured Flower: Anemone

Also known as "Windflowers," they represent anticipation and protection against evil.

Native to temperate zones worldwide (Europe/Asia/North America), they have roots in Greek mythology.

Featured Flower: Aster

Asters look like miniature stars and symbolize patience and daintiness.

Native to North America and Eurasia, the name refers to the star-like shape of the head.

Featured Flower: Gardenia

Elegant and creamy white, Gardenias symbolize secret love and purity.

Native to tropical regions of Asia and Africa, they are popular for corsages and floating bowls.

Featured Flower: Bird of Paradise

Resembling a brightly colored bird in flight, this flower represents joyfulness.

Native to South Africa, it is also known as the Crane Flower and is related to bananas.

Featured Flower: Magnolia

Magnolias are ancient flowers representing nobility, perseverance, and dignity.

Native to the Americas and Southeast Asia, they evolved to be pollinated by beetles.

Featured Flower: Begonia

Begonias represent deep thoughts and are often given as a warning of future misfortune.

Native to moist tropical climates in the Americas, Africa, and Asia.

Featured Flower: Pansy

Pansies are charming "face" flowers that symbolize remembrance and togetherness.

Native to Europe and Western Asia, the name comes from the French word for "thought."

Featured Flower: Foxglove

Tall and bell-shaped, Foxgloves symbolize both insincerity and healing.

Native to Europe, Asia, and Northwestern Africa, they are poisonous but used in medicine.

Featured Flower: Gerbera

Gerberas are the fifth most popular flower in the world, representing cheerfulness.

Native to South Africa, they act as natural air filters by removing toxins.

Featured Flower: Lilac

Lilacs represent the first emotions of love and the youthful innocence of spring.

Native to the Balkan Peninsula in Europe, they are prized for their heavy fragrance.

Featured Flower: Zinnia

Zinnias are symbols of endurance, daily remembrance, and lasting friendship.

Native to Mexico and the Southwestern United States, they were the first flowers to bloom in space.

Featured Flower: Snapdragon

Snapdragons represent graciousness and, occasionally, deception.

Native to the Mediterranean and North America, the petals "snap" when squeezed.

Featured Flower: Sweet Pea

These delicate, fragrant blooms represent blissful pleasure and departure.

Native to Sicily and Southern Italy, they were used to discover modern genetics.

Featured Flower: Wisteria

Wisteria vines symbolize longevity, devotion, and creative expansion.

Native to China, Korea, and Japan, some vines in Japan are over 150 years old.

Featured Flower: Morning Glory

Morning Glories symbolize unrequited love and the brevity of life.

Native to Tropical America, these flowers usually die by the evening of the same day.

Featured Flower: Freesia

Freesias are symbols of trust, innocence, and thoughtfulness.

Native to Southern Africa, they are highly prized in the perfume industry.

Featured Flower: Camellia

Camellias symbolize desire, passion, and refinement.

Native to East and South Asia, one species is used to produce almost all tea.

Featured Flower: Bleeding Heart

This heart-shaped flower represents unconditional love and deep compassion.

Native to Northern China, Korea, and Japan, it is linked to tales of unrequited love.

Featured Flower: Azalea

Azaleas are known as "thinking of you" flowers, representing temperance.

Native to Asia, Europe, and North America, they are the "royalty of the garden."

Featured Flower: Forget-Me-Not

Small but powerful, these flowers symbolize true love and memories.

Native to Europe and Asia, legends say a tiny plant cried out for God to remember it.

Featured Flower: Primrose

The Primrose signifies that "I can't live without you."

Native to Western and Southern Europe, it is often the first flower to bloom after winter.

Featured Flower: Gladiolus

Tall and sharp-leaved, these flowers symbolize strength and moral integrity.

Native to South and Central Africa, its name comes from the Latin word for "sword."

Featured Flower: Heather

Heather represents admiration, solitude, and good luck.

Native to Europe and Asia Minor, white heather is considered very lucky in Scotland.

Featured Flower: Hyacinth

Hyacinths are associated with playfulness and the sports of Apollo.

Native to the Eastern Mediterranean, their fragrance is incredibly strong and fills rooms.

Featured Flower: Amaryllis

Amaryllis signifies splendid beauty and worth beyond beauty.

Native to South Africa, these indoor winter bloomers can grow up to an inch a day!

Featured Flower: Edelweiss

The flower of the Alps, it symbolizes rugged purity and deep devotion.

Native to high-altitude regions of Europe, it is a sign of great bravery to find one.

Featured Flower: Ranunculus

With tissue-paper petals, Ranunculus says, "I am dazzled by your charms."

Native to the Mediterranean and Western Asia, its name means "little frog" in Latin.

Featured Flower: Calendula

Commonly known as Pot Marigold, it represents joy and health.

Native to Southern Europe and the Mediterranean, it is used for healing skin balms.

Featured Flower: Statice

Statice is the flower of "remembrance" and "success."

Native to coastal regions worldwide, its paper-like flowers retain color when dried.

Featured Flower: Stock

Stock flowers symbolize a happy life and contented existence.

Native to the Mediterranean and South Africa, they have a spicy, clove-like scent.

Featured Flower: Baby's Breath

Often a filler, this flower itself symbolizes everlasting love and purity.

Native to Eurasia and Africa, it represents the breath of the Holy Spirit in folklore.

Featured Flower: Cosmos

Cosmos represent order, peace, and serenity in the garden.

Native to Mexico, the name comes from the Greek word for "harmony."

Featured Flower: Larkspur

Larkspur signifies an open heart and strong attachment.

Native to the Northern Hemisphere and Africa, it is named after the spur at the back.

Featured Flower: Columbine

Columbine represents folly but also the spirit of the dove.

Native to temperate regions of the Northern Hemisphere, its shape looks like five doves.
